messages.content:
Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)

Is giving an error in the following code


for this code
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
If Target.Column = 5 Then
Dim rowValues As Range
Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)
If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
Dim mValues As Variant
mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
Dim i As Integer
For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value
Next i
Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
End If
End If
End Sub

The error is likely caused because the Range("B:L" & Target.Row) expression is missing a ":" between the "L" and the Target.Row reference. Try changing the line to:

Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row) 

to 

Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)
the solution you have displayed is exactly the same as the error line I provided

In that case, the error message may depend on the context where the line of code is used. Here are a few possible reasons why the line could be producing an error:

- If Target.Row is not a valid row number, the concatenation with "B:L" could produce an invalid range reference, leading to a "Run-time error '1004': Application-defined or object-defined error". To check if this is the case, you can add a debug print statement before the line to see the value of Target.Row.

- If the code is used in a sheet that is not the active sheet, the Range("B:L") reference will default to the active sheet, which may not have a range that matches this reference. To avoid this, you can fully qualify the range reference with the sheet name, like Worksheets("Sheet1").Range("B:L" & Target.Row).

- If the code is used in a workbook that is not the active workbook, the Range() method might not work as expected, and you could get a "Run-time error '438': Object doesn't support this property or method". To solve this, you can fully qualify the Range() method with the workbook object that contains the range, like Workbooks("Book1.xlsx").Worksheets("Sheet1").Range("B:L" & Target.Row)

Please have a look at the code again: Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
If Target.Column = 5 Then
Dim rowValues As Range
Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)
If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
Dim mValues As Variant
mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
Dim i As Integer
For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value
Next i
Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
End If
End If

End Sub

You are correct, the line:

Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)

is correct and should not produce an error. 

One possible reason for the error could be if the worksheet or workbook does not exist, or is hidden or protected, which might cause the Range() method to fail. 

Another reason could be if the code is used in a non-standard module, like a class module or a form module, where the Range() method might not be available.

If the error message provides more information, such as an error code or message, or a line number, that could help troubleshoot the issue further.

Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)

Is stopping the following code from running


for this code
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
If Target.Column = 5 Then
Dim rowValues As Range
Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)
If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
Dim mValues As Variant
mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
Dim i As Integer
For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value
Next i
Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
End If
End If
End Sub

If you are getting an error with the line `Set rowValues = Range("B:L" & Target.Row)` then it's possible the problem may be due to the fact that the Target.Row value is not a valid row number in your worksheet. For example, if Target.Row is 0 or less than 0, or greater than the number of rows in your worksheet, then it will cause an error. 

You can try debugging this by using the Immediate Window in the VBA editor and typing `Debug.Print Target.Row` to make sure the row value is what you expect.

Another potential issue is if the code is running in a sheet that is not your intended sheet. In this case you may want to fully qualify the Range() calls with the worksheet you intend the code to be running in, like `Set rowValues = Worksheets("Sheet1").Range("B:L" & Target.Row)`.

Finally, if the Range() call is not working properly, it's possible that your workbook may have corruption issues. In that case you may try to recreate the worksheet or even saving the workbook as a new file might fix it.
Can I use rowValues = Worksheets.ActiveSheet.Range("B:L" & Target.Row)

Yes, you can use `rowValues = Worksheets.ActiveSheet.Range("B:L" & Target.Row)` to qualify the worksheet where the range is located. However, note that using `ActiveSheet` may lead to errors if the code is called from a different worksheet than intended, or if the users navigate to a different worksheet while the code is running. This can cause the active sheet to change and lead to unexpected results in the code.

To avoid this, you can either:

1. Use the explicit worksheet name in the Range() call, like `Worksheets("Sheet1").Range("B:L" & Target.Row)` if you know the exact sheet where the code should run.

2. Use the `Target.Worksheet` object to reference the worksheet where the Target cell is located, like `rowValues = Target.Worksheet.Range("B:L" & Target.Row)`. This ensures that the range is always referred to within the same worksheet as the Target cell that triggered the change event, regardless of which sheet is active.

3. Use a conditional statement to make sure the right worksheet is active before running the rest of the code, like:

If Target.Worksheet.Name = "Sheet1" Then
    ' rest of the code goes here
End If

This ensures that the code only runs on the intended sheet, and prompts the user or handles the situation appropriately if they try to run the code on a different sheet.
the error says method or data member not found

The error "Method or data member not found" usually means that the VBA compiler cannot find a particular method or property that you have referenced in your code. Here are a few possible reasons for this error:

1. The property or method does not exist: Double-check the name of the property or method to make sure you have spelled it correctly in your code. Look it up in the object browser to see if it exists, and what objects support it.

2. The object does not support the property or method: Make sure that the property or method you are trying to access is supported by the object you are working with. For example, if you are trying to access a property that only exists for charts, but you are working with a worksheet object, you will get this error.

3. The object is not declared or initialized: In some cases, you may need to declare or initialize an object before you can use it. Make sure that all objects you are using in your code have been properly declared and initialized.

4. The correct reference libraries are not selected: If you are using external libraries or add-ins, make sure that you have the correct reference libraries selected in the VBA project. Go to the VBA editor, open the tools menu, select the References option, and look for any references with "MISSING" in front of them.

Without seeing the entire code, it's difficult to pinpoint the exact error. You may want to try debugging the code and placing breakpoints to see where the error is occurring.

No missing objects. Can you please look at the code again:  Dim rowValues As Range
Set rowValues = Worksheets.ActiveSheet.Range("B:L" & Target.Row)
If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
Dim mValues As Variant
mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
Dim i As Integer
For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value
Next i
Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
End If

In that case, the issue might be related to the line `Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value`. 

The error "Method or data member not found" sometimes occurs when the Range() method is called with incorrect arguments or is used in a wrong context. 

Check if the following steps help you to resolve the issue:

1. Verify that the cells in columns M to X (12 columns) are within the used range of the worksheet. If these columns are not included in the worksheet's used range, they are not selectable by VBA. To include them in the used range, select the cells and press Ctrl + Shift + RightArrow to select all the following columns, and then press Ctrl + Shift + DownArrow to select all the following rows. Finally, press Ctrl + Shift + End to move the cursor to the last cell of the selection, and press Delete to clear the contents. This should reset the used range of the worksheet, and allow VBA to access the cells in columns M to X.

2. Check if there is any conditional formatting applied in the worksheet that could affect the behavior of the Range() method, or if there are any merged cells or form controls that could interfere with the VBA code. These issues may not be visible on the worksheet, so it's worth checking if any unusual formatting or settings are enabled.

3. Try using the With statement to qualify the Range() calls to ensure they are working on the right worksheet, like:

```
With Worksheets("Sheet1")
    .Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 12).Value = .Range("A" & i & ":L" & i).Value
End With
```

This should ensure that the ranges are being accessed from the intended worksheet specified in the With statement.

this code is not working: Sub ColumnList()

Dim Target As Range
Dim rowValues As Range
Set rowValues = Range("B" & Target.Row & ":L")
If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
    Dim mValues As Variant
    mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
    Dim i As Integer
    For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
        Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 11).Value = Range("B" & i & ":L" & i).Value
    Next i
    Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
End If

End Sub

The issue with this code is that the Target object is not initialized in the code. The Target object needs to refer to a valid range object before it can be used to reference the Target range within the worksheet.

One way to initialize the Target object could be to call the sub routine from within another event handling procedure, like the Worksheet_SelectionChange event.

The Worksheet_SelectionChange event occurs whenever the user changes the selection within a worksheet, so you could use this event to initialize the Target object based on the selected cell.

Here's an example:

Sub ColumnList()
    Dim Target As Range
    Set Target = ActiveSheet.Selection
    
    Dim rowValues As Range
    Set rowValues = Range("B" & Target.Row & ":L" & Target.Row)
    
    If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
        Dim mValues As Variant
        mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
        Dim i As Integer
        For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
            Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 11).Value = Range("B" & i & ":L" & i).Value
        Next i
        Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
    End If
End Sub

This code initializes the Target object based on the active selection within the worksheet. You can call this routine from within the Worksheet_SelectionChange event by adding the following line to the event handler:

Private Sub Worksheet_SelectionChange(ByVal Target As Range)
    ColumnList
End Sub

Alternatively, you can pass the Target range as an argument to the ColumnList sub, like this:

Sub ColumnList(Target As Range)
    Dim rowValues As Range
    Set rowValues = Range("B" & Target.Row & ":L" & Target.Row)
    
    If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
        Dim mValues As Variant
        mValues = Application.Transpose(rowValues)
        Dim i As Integer
        For i = 1 To UBound(mValues)
            Range("M" & i).Resize(1, 11).Value = Range("B" & i & ":L" & i).Value
        Next i
        Range("M" & Target.Row).Value = mValues
    End If
End Sub

Then, you can call the ColumnList sub from within another event handler, like the Worksheet_Change event, by passing the Target object as an argument:

Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    If Target.Column = 5 Then
        ColumnList Target
    End If
End Sub

This will initialize the Target object with the specified range, allowing you to reference it within the ColumnList subroutine.
Your solution kind of worked. It transposed all the values from B1 to my last Row L13 into the sheet starting at column M.

I'm glad that the solution worked to transpose the values from columns B to L and paste them to columns M to X. However, if the transposed values start at row 1 in column M, that means the loop is not referencing the Target object correctly.

Here's the updated code that should work correctly with the Target object:

Sub ColumnList(Target As Range)
    Dim rowValues As Range
    Set rowValues = Range("B" & Target.Row & ":L" & Target.Row)
    If Not Intersect(Target, rowValues) Is Nothing Then
        Dim mValues As Variant
        mValues = Application.Transpose(Range("B" & Target.Row & ":L" & Target.Row))
        Dim i As Integer
        For i = 1 To UBound(mValues, 1)
            Range("M" & i).Value = mValues(i, 1)
        Next i
    End If
End Sub

In this updated code, the loop assigns the transposed values to cells in column M only from the top row to the last row of the transposed array. By assigning the row number with the loop counter variable, it correctly pastes the transposed values starting from the row of the selected cell.

Make sure to call this routine from within the Worksheet_Change event, using the following statement:

```
If Target.Column = 5 Then
    ColumnList Target
End If
``` 

This should ensure that the ColumnList subroutine is called only when column E changes, and that the Target object correctly refers to the cell that triggered the Worksheet_Change event.

Let me know if you have any other questions or concerns.
Thanks it worked
